text:
Consider the circle.

I want to argue for it not because it is obvious, though it is, but because its satisfaction survives scrutiny. Most pleasures dim when you look closely; the circle deepens. A square reveals its corners to be arbitrary decisions, four little verdicts about where a line should stop. A triangle is an argument, always tense, always insisting. But the circle makes no decisions at all. It is the shape that results when no point is permitted to claim privilege over any other, when every direction is treated with perfect equanimity. Democracy made geometry.

There is something restful in that fairness. The eye, traveling its edge, never arrives anywhere and never wants to; there is no terminus to dread, no corner where the gaze must brake and turn. The circle is the only shape you can fall asleep tracing. It is motion that has reconciled itself to staying home.

And it is honest about infinity in a way other figures pretend not to need. Hidden inside it is pi, that endless unrepeating tail of digits, proof that even the simplest closed curve we can draw keeps a secret too long to finish telling. A child can make one with a string and a stick; mathematicians have chased its constant for millennia and will not stop. That such modesty conceals such depth seems to me the essence of the satisfying: a thing that gives itself wholly to the glance and yet withholds something forever.

The world keeps confirming the preference. The sun, the iris, the ripple widening from a dropped stone, the orbit, the wedding ring whose meaning is precisely that it does not end. We did not invent the circle's authority; we noticed it.

So I choose the circle, the shape that asks nothing, excludes no one, and remains, after all our looking, quietly inexhaustible. Satisfaction, I think, is just completion that refuses to grow stale.
